games.explanation: Gavin Williams gets a shiny ERA from Cleveland's defense and a friendly park, but the underlying profile is a walk machine who lets righties square him up, and Tampa happens to run a lineup full of righties who do exactly that. Caminero, Aranda, and Díaz are the kind of hard-contact bats that turn an 11.7% walk rate into a long inning, and Williams has handed righties a .346 wOBA all year. Rasmussen is the better pitcher in this game by a comfortable margin, working with cleaner command and a sub-.300 xwOBA-against that Cleveland's punchless middle order has no real answer for. Getting the better team, the better starter, and the better lineup at even money is the whole pitch.

Eovaldi looks like a problem on paper until you remember who he has to navigate, and the top of this Yankee order is built specifically to make a contact-and-command righty sweat. Judge gets the platoon edge he eats for breakfast, Bellinger has quietly been a different hitter against right-handers, and Ben Rice is sitting on a .412 xwOBA versus that side that nobody seems to be pricing in. Texas counters with a lineup where Seager is the only bat keeping pace, and a closed roof neutralizes the one park quirk that helps Eovaldi survive. At -118 in a game our number says should be closer to a coin flip tilted clearly New York's way, the juice is doing less work than the matchup.

Cristopher Sanchez is a buzzsaw against lefties and a regular pitcher against everyone else, and the Giants happen to be running out a lineup full of everyone else. Chapman, Schmitt, and the rest of San Francisco's right-handed core get the version of Sanchez who allows a .289 xwOBA, not the highlight reel that buries southpaws. Webb matches him pitch for pitch in a park playing cool with the wind blowing in, which is the kind of low-scoring fight where the better starter and the deeper righty bench tend to decide things late. The market has this as a coin flip; we have the Giants closer to 52 percent. Plus money on the side with the structural edge is the entire argument.

David Peterson's platoon split is the kind of thing the market keeps ignoring until it stops working, and tonight he runs into a Washington lineup built almost entirely from the side that punishes him. Right-handed bats have tagged him for a .353 xwOBA, and James Wood in particular is the exact profile (loud contact, no fear of a walk count) that turns Peterson's command issues into crooked numbers. Cavalli isn't an ace, but Soto aside, the Mets lineup behind him doesn't scare anyone into pitching around the zone. The Nats are the better team by Elo and the better matchup on paper, and +140 is pricing them like neither is true.

Wacha's surface numbers look fine until you remember he's a contact pitcher walking into a lineup built to punish exactly that profile. The Athletics stack lefty power up top, and Nick Kurtz against right-handed pitching has been a different animal entirely, posting a .423 xwOBA with barrel rates that belong in a video game. Severino isn't an ace, but he doesn't need to be in a game the sim sees as a coin flip the market has tilted toward Kansas City for no obvious reason. At -115 on a side projecting closer to 59 percent, you're getting the better roster at a price that assumes the opposite.
